coredata.dc:title: Graphene quantum dots-crosslinked gelatin via the efficient Ugi four-component reaction: Safe photoluminescent implantable carriers for the pH-responsive delivery of doxorubicin
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2021 Acta Materialia Inc. Published by Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.
coredata.prism:publisher: Acta Materialia Inc. Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.dc:creator: Javanbakht, Siamak
Nabi, Mohadese
Shaabani, Ahmad
coredata.dc:description: In this work, citric acid-based graphene quantum dots (GQDs) were used for crosslinking gelatin (G) via the Ugi four-component reaction (Ugi-4CR) with different ratios of Ugi-4CR reactants ratios as an innovative facile, green, and straightforward method. The synthesized GQDs-crosslinked gelatin (GQD-G) hydrogels were analyzed by XRD, FT-IR, NMR, PL, SEM, and Zeta potential techniques. To explain the productivity of synthesized hydrogels as a pH-responsive drug delivery system, doxorubicin (DOX) as a model anticancer drug was loaded into them through the soaking in drug solution. The results of in-vitro swelling and drug release studies revealed dependency on the time, pH, and degree of Ugi-4CR crosslinking. GQD-G hydrogel with 0.25 mmol Ugi-4CR reactant exhibited a higher pH responsivity. It was showed a low release rate at simulated physiological environmental (pH 7.4, 37°C), but it is raised at tumor tissue conditions (pH 4.5, 41°C), extending and sustaining the delivery of DOX. Importantly, by raising the Ugi-4CR-crosslinked contents, the MTT assay exhibits a good cytocompatibility and significant cytotoxicity against breast cancer cell line (MCF-7) for the blank and DOX-loaded GQD-G, respectively. From the obtained results, the synthesized cytocompatible photoluminescent GQD-G hydrogels with suitable pH responsivity have the potential to be utilized as an implantable anticancer and bio-detection agent.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Drug delivery
Gelatin
Graphene quantum dots
Multicomponent reactions
Photoluminescent

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: DOX loading into the GQD-G hydrogels

xocs:cp-notices.xocs:cp-notice: © 2021 Acta Materialia Inc. Published by Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Research Council of Shahid Beheshti University

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This paper has been supported by the Research Council of Shahid Beheshti University and the RUDN University Strategic Academic Leadership Program (A. Shaabani).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration of the constructing photoluminescent GQD-G hydrogel through the Ugi-4CR method.
The loading capacity of DOX into the GQD-G hydrogels (A) and zeta potential of the GQD-G hydrogels in distilled water at 25°C (n=3, *P < 0.05).
FT-IR spectra for the GQD-G 0.25 (A), GQD-G 0.05 (B), GQD-G 1.00 (C), DOX@GQD-G 0.25 (D), DOX@GQD-G 0.50 (E), and DOX@GQD-G 1.00 (F).
Dispersion 1H NMR spectrum of GQD-G 1.00 in DMSO–d 6.
Dispersion PL spectra for GQD-G 0.25, GQD-G 0.05, GQD-G 1.00 in distilled water with related photographic images under sunlight and UV 365 nm lamp.
SEM images of the GQD-G 0.25 (A), GQD-G 0.05 (B), and GQD-G 1.00 (C) with related higher magnification SEM images A′, B′, and C′, respectively. The images were edited by the color filter.
Swelling profile of GQD-G hydrogel at PBS (pH 7.4, 37°C) (A) and SAB (pH 4.5, 41°C) (B) media (n=3, **P < 0.05).
DOX release profile of GQD-G hydrogels at PBS (pH 7.4, 37°C) (A) and SAB (pH 4.5, 41°C) (B) media (n=3, ***P < 0.05).
Results of MTT assay on MCF-7 cells after being 48 incubation time with different concentrations of blank and DOX-loaded GQD-G hydrogels (n=3, # P < 0.05).
The PLQYs of the synthesized GQD-G hydrogels.
The results of the kinetics models for the DOX release from the GQD-G hydrogels.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: Notes: Linear regression is used to achieve the parameters of kinetic models. Where F is the drug released fraction up to time t. The parameters of k0, kf, and kH are the related kinetic constant. Ln: natural logarithm.

head.ce:title: Graphene quantum dots-crosslinked gelatin via the efficient Ugi four-component reaction: Safe photoluminescent implantable carriers for the pH-responsive delivery of doxorubicin

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Organic Chemistry, Shahid Beheshti University, Daneshjou Boulevard Street, Tehran, 1983969411, Iran
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: aDepartment of Organic Chemistry, Shahid Beheshti University, Daneshjou Boulevard Street, Tehran, 1983969411, Iran

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Рeoples’ Friendship University of Russia (RUDN University), 6, Miklukho-Maklaya Street, Moscow, 117198, Russian Federation

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Рeoples’ Friendship University of Russia (RUDN University)

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: bРeoples’ Friendship University of Russia (RUDN University), 6, Miklukho-Maklaya Street, Moscow, 117198, Russian Federation

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: Department of Organic Chemistry, Shahid Beheshti University, Daneshjou Boulevard Street, Tehran, 1983969411, Iran.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this work, citric acid-based graphene quantum dots (GQDs) were used for crosslinking gelatin (G) via the Ugi four-component reaction (Ugi-4CR) with different ratios of Ugi-4CR reactants ratios as an innovative facile, green, and straightforward method. The synthesized GQDs-crosslinked gelatin (GQD-G) hydrogels were analyzed by XRD, FT-IR, NMR, PL, SEM, and Zeta potential techniques. To explain the productivity of synthesized hydrogels as a pH-responsive drug delivery system, doxorubicin (DOX) as a model anticancer drug was loaded into them through the soaking in drug solution. The results of in-vitro swelling and drug release studies revealed dependency on the time, pH, and degree of Ugi-4CR crosslinking. GQD-G hydrogel with 0.25 mmol Ugi-4CR reactant exhibited a higher pH responsivity. It was showed a low release rate at simulated physiological environmental (pH 7.4, 37°C), but it is raised at tumor tissue conditions (pH 4.5, 41°C), extending and sustaining the delivery of DOX. Importantly, by raising the Ugi-4CR-crosslinked contents, the MTT assay exhibits a good cytocompatibility and significant cytotoxicity against breast cancer cell line (MCF-7) for the blank and DOX-loaded GQD-G, respectively. From the obtained results, the synthesized cytocompatible photoluminescent GQD-G hydrogels with suitable pH responsivity have the potential to be utilized as an implantable anticancer and bio-detection agent.

ce:section.ce:para: The development of new bioactive molecules as a drug is time-consuming and high-pricing. Recent research revealed that fabricating efficient platforms as drug delivery systems (DDSs) can respond to these problems [1,2]. DDSs with controlling drug release profiles in desired sites have the potential to manage the drug dosage rate, enhance the drug's bioavailability and decrease the drug's possible side effects [3]. However, a great challenge is designing innovative DDSs with a more tunable and predictable release profile [4]. On the other hand, protection of the body organs and tissues from the probable toxicity before achieving the drugs to the targeted locations is of great significance. In this regard, stimuli-responsive DDSs based on naturally-derived polymers concerned great attention in recent years, which has been usually developed upon the hydrogel systems [5,6].

Among naturally-derived polymers, gelatin-based carriers as the talented DDSs concerned great importance due to some key features, i.e., their great biodegradability and biocompatibility in physiological media [7, 8]. Thanks to its high availability and inexpensiveness, gelatin is broadly used in pharmaceutical and biomedical applications, documenting as a Generally Regarded As Safe (GRAS) material for drug administration [9]. The amino acid arrangements in the structure of gelatin, Arg-Gly-Asp (RGD), act as the cell-recognition motifs and improve the bioactivity of systems in comparison with other polymers that shortage these structures [10,11]. Alternatively, gelatin-based hydrogels as a carrier for DDSs have been established to be efficient due to their exclusive features which can load a variety of water-soluble and non-soluble drugs [12]. Hydrogels are linear or branched hydrophilic 3D cross-linked polymers, which can swell several times in contact with moister, saline, water, or physiological media [13,14]. In light of the great applicability of gelatin-hydrogels as DDSs, they have been commonly employed in the wide-ranging of applications, for example, tissue engineering [15], cancer therapy [16], and therapeutic angiogenesis [17]. Recent reports in the literature have deliberated on utilizing modified gelatin to construct well-organized drug carriers [18, 19]. Improving the drug bioavailability [20], drug loading capacity [21], and targeting ability are some of the important subjects, achieving by modification of gelatin with divers materials [22]. Particularly, nanoparticles showed interesting features in this contribution and have been frequently employed in designing gelatin nanocomposites for desired DDSs [23,24].

Recently, graphene-based nanomaterials are attracting more and more consideration. They have been extensively employed in different applications, i.e., supercapacitors [25,26], electronic [27,28], sensor [29], and medicine [30]. Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) are graphene fragments smaller than 20 nm lateral dimension [31]. To date, two main methods are considered for the synthesis of GQDs from various carbon precursors that are in general categorized into “Bottom-up” and “Top-down” approaches [32,33]. As exclusive nanoparticles, they have great characteristics, such as chemical inertness, easy adjustability of shape and size, edges functional groups, electrons delocalizing in a high surface area, suitable emission, unparalleled excitation, exclusive fundamental fluorescence, dissolution in a variety of solvents, and π-π interactions ability with different biomolecules [30,34,35]. Particularly, GQDs obtained from biomaterials, i.e., citric acid, have gained immense attention among researchers due to their abundance, eco-friendly nature, sustainability, diverse functionality, aqueous solubility, and biocompatibility [36,37]. Citric acid, a tribasic acid, is typically derived from citrus fruits and manufactured more than two million tons every year. The structures of citric acid-based GQDs are constituted with “carbon”, “oxygen” and “hydrogen” elements, which have shown lower cytotoxicity than that of inorganic quantum dots, which may release toxic metal ions after degradation [38–40]. Thanks to these significant features, GQDs were considered talented nanomaterials for modifying different materials [41]. While different approaches have been described for the modification of gelatin with quantum dots to prepare hydrogel systems [42,43], the development of a green, facile, and efficient strategy is a great challenge.

In the recent decade, multicomponent reactions (MCRs) as an advanced tool offer great promises to prepare diverse functional hydrogels [44]. They have been well-known as an advanced sustainable tool for the construction of multifunctional compounds due to their one-pot synthesis procedure, high atom efficiency, and high productivity [45,46]. These important make adequate some of the green chemistry aspects such as reduced cost, low pollutant, and waste creations [47]. On the other hand, our recent progress in materials chemistry reveals the efficiency of the MCRs approaches in the construction of biomedical platforms [48,49]. The main substances of MCRs, i.e., amine, carbonyl, or carboxylic acid functional groups, typically exist or simply be created in the structure of (nano)materials. The abundant carboxylic functional groups on the GQDs surface and desired amine functional groups in the gelatin structure make them talented candidates to participate in MCRs and construct greatly talented multifunctional crosslinked materials. It should be maintained that the MCRs method as the modification or crosslinking strategy for the gelatin biopolymer was rarely considered in the literature [50].

Up to now, most reported anticancer drug carriers rely on the transforming drug through the oral or injection, but offering implantable systems directly in touch with the targeted cells are rarely considered in this field [51]. Therefore, it is important to introduce an alternative vehicle that can deliver anticancer drugs effectively in the targeted tumor tissue. In this respect, based on our previous findings in modification/functionalization of materials via MCRs [44,50,52,53], herein, we report a green, straightforward, and efficient novel method to prepare GQDs-crosslinked gelatin (GQD-G) hydrogels through the Ugi-4CR method. For the evaluation of the productivity of the synthesized novel GQD-G hydrogels as a pH-responsive drug delivery system, doxorubicin (DOX), a typical anticancer drug prescript as an agent in chemotherapy, was loaded through the immersing of hydrogels in its standard solution. Then in-vitro drug release investigation was considered in the simulated physiological and cancer tissue conditions. Besides, their cytotoxicity against breast cancer cell lines (MCF-7) was evaluated. Obtained results enabled us to understand the potential of GQD-G hydrogels as efficient DDSs for local administration. Moreover, this multifunctional system, GQD-G hydrogels, with great photoluminescent and swelling performance has the potential to be employed in other applications of hydrogel-based materials such as selective adsorption, sensors, and bio-imaging.

Citric acid monohydrate, gelatin, cyclohexyl isocyanide, 4-bromobenzaldehyde, and all other materials and solvents were obtained from Sigma-Aldrich Co. and utilized in analytical grade without any purification. All biological compounds were purchased from Invitrogen (USA). Doxorubicin hydrochloride (DOX) was obtained from Behbod Darou (Iran) and all biological compounds were purchased from Invitrogen (USA).

Fourier transform infrared (FT–IR) and UV–vis absorption spectra of the samples were recorded on an FT–IR spectrometer equipped with ATR (Thermo Nicolet, model iS50, USA) and UV–vis spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, model 1700, Japan), respectively. The zeta potential was determined using DLS-ZP/Particle Sizer (Malvern model MAL1032660, England). The PL spectra were obtained by a spectrofluorometric (PerkinElmer, LS45, Germany). 1H NMR spectra were obtained by Avance spectrometer (Bruker DRX-30072, 300.13 MHz, deuterated dimethyl sulfoxide, DMSO-d 6). The scanning electron microscope (SEM) was recorded with an electron microscope (FEI ESEM QUANTA 200 model, USA).

GQDs were synthesized according to our previous report through direct pyrolysis of citric acid [54]. In a typical experiment, 200 mg citric acid was heated up to 200°C with a mantle. After 35 min, the obtained orange liquid was drop-wisely added into 5 mL of methanol (MeOH) under vigorous stirring. The stock solution of GQDs was achieved and stored in the refrigerator for further use.

For the preparation of GQD-G hydrogels with different chemical crosslinking ratios (GQD-G 0.25, GQD-G 0.50, and GQD-G 1.00), 100 mg of the high molecular weight gelatin (see the supplementary material) were individually dispersed in the prepared stock solution of GQDs containing different amounts of cyclohexyl isocyanide and 4-bromobenzaldehyde (0.25, 0.50 and 1.00 mmol) in a closed vessel. Then, the reaction mixtures were stirred at 60°C for 12 h. After completion of the reactions, the supernatant of each reaction mixture was discarded and the precipitate was collected and washed three times with water:methanol (80:20) and dried at ambient temperature.

To evaluate the swelling performance of the prepared GQD-G hydrogels, the in-vitro swelling study was achieved as a previously reported procedure [50]. Typically, 100 mg of the GQD-G hydrogels was immersed in 10 mL of phosphate-buffered saline (PBS, pH 7.4, 37°C) and sodium acetate buffered (SAB, pH 4.5, 41°C) solutions. At certain time intervals, the swelled hydrogels were taken out and weighed after removing surface absorbed water by blotting onto the filter paper. The swelling ratio was calculated with the Eq. (1):
(1)
S w e l l i n g
r a t e =
(
W s
−
W i
)
W i
where, Wi and Ws are the weights of samples at initial and after swelling, respectively.

For the loading of DOX into the GQD-G, the same mass (100 mg) from each hydrogel was soaked in 10 mL of DOX stock solution (100 ppm) under continuous shaking for 72 h in the dark. The supernatant of the loading mixtures was analyzed using UV-vis spectrophotometer to measure the amount of unloaded DOX and followed by their loading capacity. DOX-loaded samples were collected and washed with distilled water to remove unloaded drugs and dried at ambient temperature.

The in-vitro release profile of DOX was carried out by immersing 10 mg of DOX-loaded hydrogels in 10 mL of phosphate-buffered saline (PBS, pH 7.4, 37°C) and sodium acetate buffered (SAB, pH 4.5, 41°C) solutions to simulate physiological environment and tumor tissue conditions, respectively. At certain time intervals, the supernatant of release media (2 mL) was taken out and replaced with aliquots fresh buffer. The taken samples were considered using UV-vis spectrophotometer. The calibration curve of the standard DOX at λ=483 nm was used to calculate the DOX loading capacity and cumulative release profile by the following formula, Eq. (2) and Eq. (3), respectively:
(2)
D r u g
l o a d i n g
capacity
( % )
=
w e i g h t
o f
d r u g
i n
t h e
s u b s t r a t e
w e i g h t
o f
t h e
i n i t i a l
d r u g
×
100
(3)
D r u g
r e l e a s e
( % )
=

a m o u n t
o f
r e l e a s e d
d r u g

a m o u n t
o f
l o a d e d
d r u g
×
100
Furthermore, the release kinetics of DOX were studied as our previously reported method (Table 1 ) [55].

The cytotoxicity of the prepared hydrogels was evaluated by MTT test against a breast cancer cell line (MCF-7). In 96-well plates, the cells were seeded at 10,000 cells•well–1 density in 200 μL Dulbecco modified Eagle medium (DMEM; Gibco BRL) containing 10% fetal bovine serum. The cells were treated with various concentrations of DOX, DOX-loaded GQD-G hydrogels in an equal concentration of DOX and incubated under 5% CO2 at 37°C for 48 h. In addition, the cells were treated with GQD-G hydrogels to consider their biocompatibility nature. Afterward, to solubilize the formazan crystals, 150 μL fresh culture medium having MTT solution (5 mg•mL–1) was replaced with each well and incubated at 37°C for 3 h. Then, after removing the culture medium, 200 μL dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO) was added to each well. The optical density values of the plate were determined using a microplate reader (BioTek, Bad Friedrichshall, Germany) at 570 nm and the cell viability was calculated by Eq. (4).
(4)
Cell
viability
( % )
=
Mean
absorbance
of
each
group
Mean
absorbance
of
control
group
×
100

For the results, data are presented as mean ± standard deviation. Statistical analysis was achieved using Microsoft Excel software by one-way analysis of variance (ANOVA), in which statistical significance was measured for a P < 0.05. A minimum of three experiments was considered for each test.

In the chemistry of DDSs, a significant challenge is to fabricate carriers with high bioavailability, biocompatibility, stability, and environmentally friendly features, which can recurrently be attained by the creation of bioactive frameworks through the most MCRs protocol [56,57]. Significantly, Ugi four-component reaction (Ugi-4CR) as one of the most standing respect to organic name reactions allow the construction of bioactive pseudo-peptide skeleton. It provides a great opportunity for materials development in medicinal chemistry owing to their outstanding bioactivity [53]. In this regard, based on our previous findings in modified/functionalized materials via MCRs [57,58], we report a green, straightforward, and efficient alternative method to prepare photoluminescent GQD-G hydrogels through the Ugi-4CR method (Scheme 1 ). GQDs were first obtained from citric acid through the direct pyrolysis method (Scheme S1, see the supplementary material), which was fully characterized in our previous report [59]. Then, the abundant carboxylic functional groups on the GQDs surface enabled us to participate them in Ugi-4CR with the commercially available starting materials, including gelatin, cyclohexyl isocyanide, and 4-bromobenzaldehyde. The proposed mechanism for the construction of GQD-G hydrogels is illustrated in Scheme S2 (see the supplementary material). Moreover, different concentrations of cyclohexyl isocyanide and 4-bromobenzaldehyde reactants were considered to elucidate the effect of crosslinking ratio and also the type of crosslinking on the properties of hydrogels. In a low concentration of Ugi-4CR reactants, it can also be possible to generate an electrostatic interaction (physical crosslinking) among the amine groups of gelatin and carboxylate moieties of GQDs as illustrated in Scheme 1. This procedure, Ugi-4CR, has the potential to enhance the bioavailability of materials since the generation of an additional bioactive framework, carboxamide moieties, in the structure of gelatin during its crosslinking with GQDs [50,52]. To study the successful preparation of GQD-G hydrogels and their applicability as a DOX delivery system, they were considered as the following investigations.

ce:section.ce:section-title: DOX loading into the GQD-G hydrogels
ce:section.ce:para: In this work, we select DOX as the model anticancer drug to load into the synthesized GQD-G hydrogels. According to our previous report [55], the loading process was carried out through the immersing of hydrogels into the drug solution. The drug loading capacity of GQD-G 0.25, GQD-G 0.05, GQD-G 1.00 was determined to be 52.5 %, 66.9 %, and 70.9 % using UV–vis spectroscopy, respectively ( Fig. 1A). As the drug loading protocol is performed in a distilled water, the possible interactions between DOX and GQD-G hydrogels can be related to the hydrogen bonding, electrostatic interaction, and π-π stacking interaction (between GQDs and DOX) [44]. The zeta potential of analyte (DOX) and absorbent (GQD-G hydrogels) has an important effect on the loading capacity. The result revealed that the zeta potential of GQD-G has decreased about 9.6 mV by increasing the ratios of Ugi-4CR reactants (cyclohexyl isocyanide and 4-bromobenzaldehyde). This difference in the zeta potential affects the loading capacity of GQD-G hydrogels as the primary amine of DOX (pKa ∼ 7.2-8.2) is positively charged in distilled water.

To understand the banding interaction of the synthesized hydrogels, the FT-IR spectra of gelatin, GQD-G hydrogels, and DOX-loaded GQD-G hydrogels were considered ( Fig. 2). FT-IR spectrum of pure gelatin appeared the absorption bands at 1728 and 3300 cm−1 that respectively related to the COO and O/N-H stretching bands (Fig. S1, see the supplementary material). Besides, a multipeak absorption pattern in the region between 1350 and 1650 cm−1 ascribed the protein backbone of gelatin. In the GQD-G hydrogels’ spectra, the peaks that confirm the successful carboxamide functionalization are the presence of C-Br at around 650 cm−1 (strong), amide III at 1328 cm−1 (weak), C=C aromatic at 1445 cm−1, amide II at 1528 cm−1 (medium), amide I at 1620 cm−1 (strong), C-H stretching of the methyl and methylene groups vibrations around 2850–2930 cm−1 (medium), and stretching of O-H and N-H around 3100–3650 cm−1 (strong) (Fig. 2). The FT-IR spectrum of GQD-G with various crosslinking ratios in comparison with pure gelatin exhibits an alteration in the peaks related to the protein backbone and also an increment in the intensity of the amide I band with increasing the concentration of the ratios of Ugi-4CR reactants. Remarkably, the characteristic bands of C=O ketone at 1725 cm−1 (strong) and C-O-C vibration band at around 1160 cm−1 related to the DOX molecule have appeared in the FT-IR spectrum of DOX@GQD-G hydrogels (Fig. 2), confirming the successful loading of DOX into the GQD-G hydrogels.

The structure of GQD-G hydrogels was evaluated by the dispersion NMR technique in DMSO-d 6 ( Fig. 3). 1H NMR spectrum of GQD-G appeared characteristic peaks describes to the methylene protons of cyclohexyl ring and gelatin (δH around 0.7–2.3 ppm), N-CH-cyclohexyl (δH around 3.8 ppm), N-CHCO (amide moiety, δH around 4.3 ppm), N-CH-benzyl (amide moiety, δH around 4.6 ppm), hydroxyl protons (δH around 6.1 ppm), NH of amide group and aromatic protons (δH around 6.5-8.2 ppm). These typical peaks evidently prove the Ugi-crosslinked product, GQD-G hydrogels.

To investigate the luminescent properties of the synthesized GQD-G hydrogels, PL spectroscopy method was utilized. As seen in Fig. 4, the maximum emission wavelength of GQD-G hydrogels was observed at 460 nm. Pure gelatin also showed photoluminescent features at 420 nm (Fig. S2, see the supplementary material). However, due to the high fundamental fluorescence feature of GQDs, it is amplified by composition with GQD, which can also visibly induce in the GQD-G (photographic images under UV 365 nm lamp in Fig. 4). Moreover, according to the reported methods in the literature, the PL quantum yields (PLQYs) of the GQD-G hydrogels at maximum emission wavelength were calculated using quinine sulfate as the reference (Table 1).53 Form the results, the PL intensity of GQD-G hydrogels is reduced by increasing the ratios of Ugi-4CR reactants in the synthesis process. For these observations, some reasons have been reported such as the self-absorbing, filter effect, inner photoluminescence reabsorption, internal or external transformations phenomena, partial quenching, extinction effect, and intersystem transitions [54]. Probably, these phenomena are more amplified with the rigidity of polymeric network, increasing by the crosslinking ratio from GQD-G 0.25 to GQD-G 1.00. However, the appropriate luminescent properties of the synthesized GQD-G hydrogels make them potential to be employed as a bio-detection application and we recommended further investigations to fully address this subject.

The size and morphology of the drug carrier are the most important factors that concern the possible administration route of the carrier. The structure and morphology of the synthesized GQD-G hydrogels were considered using the SEM technique and results are displayed in Fig. 5. As seen in Fig. 5, a highly pores structure is observable for GQD-G hydrogels, which are more altered and deformed by raising the crosslinking ratio from GQD-G 0.25 to GQD-G 1.00. In addition, the higher magnification SEM images of GQD-G hydrogels exhibit many cavities in their internal zones. This structure could result in an easier circulation of physiological media and the release of drug from the polymeric network of nanocomposite [60,61]. Due to the micro-sized construction of the synthesized hydrogels, they can be an appropriate carrier for the local administration of DOX drug through implanting in specified tissue.

The swelling performance of hydrogel not only approves the successful polymer crosslinking but also affects its application. The swelling profile of the synthesized GQD-G hydrogels at PBS (pH 7.4, 37°C) and SAB (pH 4.5, 41°C) media is displayed in Fig. 6. Typically, various factors impact the swelling rate of hydrogel like construction, degree of crosslinking, ionic strength, pH, and temperature [62]. All samples showed a time-dependent swelling manner and the swelling ratio is increased with time. Besides, they displayed a pH-dependent behavior and the swelling ratio is higher at acidic pH. Since the GQDs carboxylic groups (pka 4.7) are protonated at pH 4.5, the generated electrostatic interaction among the amines of gelatin and carboxylate groups of GQDs has deactivated; accordingly, the polymeric network tends to swell. This phenomenon is superior in GQD-G with a low content of Ugi-4CR product as its chemical crosslinking degree is lower. On the other hand, by changing the pH to 7.4 and generation physical crosslinking by forming electrostatic interaction among the GQDs and gelatin, the polymeric network tends to shrink. Among hydrogels, the sample GQD-G 0.25 exhibited a higher swelling responsivity for the alteration of pH, which is due to the more physical crosslinking ratio in its structure.

To elucidate the potential of the synthesized hydrogels as a pH-responsive anticancer carrier, in-vitro DOX release study was carried out in the mimicked physiological environment (PBS, pH 7.4, 37°C) and tumor tissue conditions (SAB, pH 4.5, 41°C), and drug release profiles are shown in Fig. 7. The results reveal a time, a degree of Ugi-4CR crosslinked product, and pH-dependent release manner. The DOX release rate is increased with time and it is higher at pH 4.5 than that of pH 7.4. This feature of GQD-G hydrogels could prevent the release of DOX in the physiological environment, reducing the possible side effects of non-specific DOX drug. Among synthesized hydrogel, GQD-G 0.25 showed a higher pH-dependent DOX release and its release is highest and lowest at pH 4.5 and pH 7.4, respectively. This can be related to the swelling features of hydrogels, exhibiting a higher sensitivity for pH from GQD-G 0.25 to GQD-G 1.00. The swelling rate can effectively control the circulation of buffered media and manage the release of DOX. Another phenomenon may be related to the freer carboxylate groups of GQDs and amine moieties of gelatin in the hydrogel structure, making an additional interaction with DOX molecules and prevent its release at pH 7.4. However, these groups have more participated in the Ugi-4CR in GQD-G 1.00 and their effects in the controlling of DOX-release are losses. Furthermore, to evaluate the release kinetics of DOX, the release data were fitted with different models according to the previously reported procedures [55], and the results are summarized in Table 2 . Among various kinetic models, Weibull is the best-fitted model for the DOX release from GQD-G hydrogels.

The cytotoxicity of the GQD-G and DOX-loaded GQD-G (DOX@GQD-G) hydrogels was evaluated using MTT assay against MCF-7 cell lines ( Fig. 8). After incubation for 48 h, obtained results exhibit a dose-dependent behavior and incubation with high-content samples causes higher cytotoxicity. At the equal concentration of DOX, the cytotoxicity of the free DOX solution is higher in comparison with DOX@GQD-G hydrogel (Fig. 8A). This is due to the slower drug release from GQD-G hydrogels, which results in their lower cytotoxicity. On the other hand, similar results in literature established that the micro-sized carriers have lower cytotoxicity than that of nano-sized carriers owing to their restriction in transmission into the cell [63,64]. The DOX release profile at tumor tissue conditions (pH 4.5, Fig. 7A) showed a higher DOX release for GQD-G 0.25 sample while its cytotoxicity is the lowest. Probably, the formation of pseudo-peptide skeletons via the Ugi-4CR during the crosslinking of gelatin with GQDs can enhance their interactions with MCF-7 cells [65,66]. It should be noticed that whereas the DOX@GQD-G hydrogel exhibited lower cytotoxicity than free DOX, it can deliver DOX at a controllable rate and reduce its possible side effect. Furthermore, remaining the cell viability over 80% for the blank GQD-G hydrogels confirmed their non-significant cytotoxicity against MCF-7 cells (Fig. 8B).

In designing polymeric nanocomposite hydrogel as a pH-responsive anticancer platform, the modification polymer during its crosslinking plays a significant role. This work deals with a new strategy based on facile, green, and straightforward Ugi four-component reaction (Ugi-4CR) for the construction of citric acid-based graphene quantum dots (GQDs)-crosslinked gelatin (GQD-G) as a pH-sensitive hydrogel. This type of processing for the modification during the construction of nanocomposite hydrogels offers feasible characteristics because of the applications of green method, i.e., the multicomponent reaction among commercially available cheap materials. On the other hand, the chemical crosslinking of gelatin with GQDs through Ugi-4CR is considered necessary as the constructing process effectively generates an additional bioactive skeleton, i.e., pseudo-peptide moieties, affecting the bioactivity and bioavailability of drugs and carriers. Besides, the high fundamental fluorescence feature of GQDs is induced in the synthesized hydrogels and they showed a desired photoluminescent performance. The swelling and doxorubicin (DOX) release analyses exhibited a pH-responsive behavior and both of them were higher at the acidic condition (pH 4.5). This feature of GQD-G hydrogels could prevent the release of DOX in the physiological environment (pH 7.4), reducing the possible side effects of non-specific DOX drug. The content of Ugi-4CR crosslinking in the construction of hydrogels influences their pH responsivity. Since this reaction was carried out among the carboxylic acid functional groups of GQDs, amine moieties of gelatin, cyclohexyl isocyanide, and 4-bromobenzaldehyde, more participation in Ugi-4CR declines the physical crosslinking (electrostatic interaction between amine and carboxylate groups) and therefore eliminates their effects in pH-sensitivity of the hydrogel. GQD-G hydrogel with 0.25 mmol Ugi-4CR reactant exhibited a higher pH responsivity. While its release rate is lowest at simulated physiological environmental (pH 7.4, 37°C), it is raised at tumor tissue conditions (pH 4.5, 41°C), extending and sustaining the delivery of DOX. On the other hand, the degree of Ugi-4CR crosslinking shows a positive effect on the cytotoxicity of the synthesized hydrogels. GQD-G 1.00 exhibited notable cytotoxicity against breast cancer cell line (MCF-7). According to the results, the designed hydrogels may attract great attention to construct a safe photoluminescent pH-responsive system, having the potential to be employed as an implantable anticancer and bio-detection agent. In addition, we believe that the presented strategy could attract much attention from the community of material chemistry for the preparation of biomedical platforms thanks to its green chemistry principle.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare no competing financial interest.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This paper has been supported by the Research Council of Shahid Beheshti University and the RUDN University Strategic Academic Leadership Program (A. Shaabani).

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:10.1016/j.mtla.2021.101233.
